doc.title: Consignment — revenue waits for the real sale
doc.event: Ursa Co. ships goods costing $40,000 to a dealer on consignment. The dealer sells all of them to customers for $60,000, keeps a 10% commission, and remits the balance. Prepare Ursa's entries.

lines.label: reclassify, don't sell
lines.why: Still the consignor's inventory — control (and risk) never transferred to the dealer. Only the shelf changed.
lines.trap: NO revenue at shipment. Consignment's entire exam existence is punishing people who book the sale here.

lines.label: off the home shelf
lines.trap: The consignee books NOTHING for inventory either — it's not theirs to record.

entries.caption: Consignee sells all of it for 60,000, keeps 10%, remits 54,000

lines.label: the net remittance
lines.why: 60,000 collected by the dealer minus their 6,000 commission.
lines.trap: Cash ≠ revenue here in the other direction: revenue is the GROSS 60,000, cash is the net.

lines.label: the dealer's cut
lines.why: The consignor is the principal — it reports gross revenue and the commission as ITS expense.
lines.trap: Netting revenue to 54,000 hides the commission and understates both revenue and expense — the principal/agent trap.

lines.label: gross — the real sale finally happened
lines.why: Revenue recognizes when the CONSIGNEE sells to the end customer — that's when control leaves the consignor.
lines.trap: The consignee (agent) reports only its 6,000 commission as revenue — never the 60,000.

lines.label: now it's sold
lines.trap: COGS waited on the consignment shelf exactly as long as revenue did.

lines.label: shelf clears
lines.why: The reclass unwinds at the true sale.
lines.trap: Any UNSOLD consigned goods stay on the consignor's balance sheet at period end — a favorite inventory-count twist.

memorize.body: Consignor: shipment = inventory reclass only; revenue (GROSS) + commission expense + COGS all trigger when the consignee sells to the end customer.
Principal vs agent: whoever CONTROLS the good before transfer is the principal (gross revenue). The agent reports only its fee. Consignment is the textbook's cleanest principal/agent case.

questions.prompt: Goods costing 40,000 are shipped on consignment. Revenue recognized by the consignor AT SHIPMENT =

distractors.feedback: Shipment transfers location, not CONTROL — no revenue until the consignee sells to an end customer. At shipment: reclass inventory, nothing more.

build.scaffold: Three beats on the consignor's books: reclass at shipment (no revenue), gross revenue + commission when the END customer buys, cost matched at the same moment.
